---
title: "Mother Lode California orogenic gold — within-region prospectivity"
subtitle: "A Random Forest on public open data over the Sierra Nevada gold belt, with the cross-region transfer test that didn't work"
date: 2026-05-11
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
---

## The experiment

California's Mother Lode is the historical gold-producing region that
extends along the Sierra Nevada foothills from Mariposa to Amador,
with a documented production history of roughly 106 million ounces
from the mid-1800s to today. We assembled a within-region
prospectivity model on this footprint using only public open data.

## Data layers

Six independent data sources, each capturing a different facet of
the geological recipe for orogenic gold:

### Lithology — SGMC canonical 33-class

The State Geologic Map Compilation provides the host-rock context.
The Sierra Nevada batholith dominates the east; accreted metamorphic
terranes (Calaveras Complex, Mariposa Slate) form the gold-bearing
units to the west.

![SGMC canonical lithology in the Mother Lode AOI](../../_freeze/notebooks/motherlode/data_exploration/figure-html/sgmc-map-output-1.png)

### Geophysics — USGS magnetic and Bouguer gravity

The basement density and magnetic susceptibility structure. Bouguer
gravity captures the deep-crust signal that distinguishes the
batholith from the surrounding accreted terranes.

![USGS Bouguer gravity, residual signature of the basement-rock density structure](../../_freeze/notebooks/motherlode/data_exploration/figure-html/grav-map-output-1.png)

![USGS magnetic residual TI](../../_freeze/notebooks/motherlode/data_exploration/figure-html/mag-map-output-1.png)

### Geochemistry — NGDB stream sediments

The National Geochemical Database carries pathfinder element
concentrations measured on stream-sediment samples taken on a
roughly 5 km grid. For orogenic gold, the pathfinder columns that
matter are Au itself, plus As, Sb, and W as classical alteration-
mineral indicators.

![NGDB stream-sediment Au, log10 scale](../../_freeze/notebooks/motherlode/data_exploration/figure-html/ngdb-au-map-output-1.png)

### Structure — SGMC mapped faults

Orogenic gold forms in quartz veins emplaced along high-angle faults
during late-stage compressional tectonics. The fault network is the
fluid-pathway feature.

![SGMC mapped faults in the AOI](../../_freeze/notebooks/motherlode/data_exploration/figure-html/faults-map-output-1.png)

### Historical occurrences — MRDS

The USGS Mineral Resources Data System carries the historical
occurrence records that serve as the positive label set. After a
Cox-Singer-aware regex filter on the free-text `dep_type` field to
remove placer, epithermal, and skarn-Au records, the cleaned label
set has 6,149 orogenic-Au occurrences inside the AOI.

![MRDS gold occurrences in the AOI](../../_freeze/notebooks/motherlode/data_exploration/figure-html/mrds-map-output-1.png)

### Terrain — 1 arc-second DEM with slope and TRI

Topography and terrain ruggedness as context layers. Useful primarily
to distinguish high-relief gold-belt geology from low-relief overburden.

![1 arc-second DEM, AOI](../../_freeze/notebooks/motherlode/data_exploration/figure-html/dem-map-output-1.png)

## How the algorithm consumes the data

The Random Forest model — an ensemble of decision trees each fit on
a bootstrap sample of the training data, with each tree split node
selected from a random subset of the feature columns — is a good fit
for this feature stack because the prediction task is exactly the
multivariate-conditional rule it is built to learn: prospective if
(specific lithology) AND (near a fault) AND (stream-sediment Au
above threshold) AND (gravity signature consistent with the host
basement). Each tree votes; the final score is the proportion of
trees voting "prospective."

We use 20-kilometer spatial-block cross-validation so the held-out
test cells are not in the same H3 super-cell as their training
neighbors, and we report AUC with bootstrap 95-percent confidence
intervals to capture sampling uncertainty within each held-out fold.

## Results

Spatial-block cross-validated AUC: **0.85 [0.79, 0.90]**.
Top 5 percent of area captures **19.5 percent** of held-out
positives. SHAP feature-attribution analysis surfaces exactly the
features a domain geologist would predict: distance to mapped
greenstone-belt boundaries, distance to high-angle faults, NGDB
arsenic and antimony as pathfinder geochemistry, and the
SGMC-canonical lithology one-hots for the Calaveras Complex and
Mariposa Slate (the two host units of the historic mines).

![Mother Lode prospectivity map, color = predicted probability of orogenic Au (Cox-Singer 36a)](../../data/derived/motherlode/fig_prospectivity_motherlode_cleaned.png)

## Interesting finding 1: SHAP attribution matches the geological recipe

When the SHAP analysis ranks features by their average effect on
model output, the top features are exactly the ones a domain
geologist would call out as the canonical orogenic-Au recipe:

- Distance to mapped greenstone-belt boundaries
- Distance to high-angle faults
- NGDB arsenic and antimony pathfinder geochemistry
- Calaveras Complex and Mariposa Slate lithology one-hots

The map highlights known producing districts at the expected
locations but also surfaces several lesser-explored areas near
Trinity County and the upper Yuba drainage that match the geological
recipe without the same historical drilling density. The result
demonstrates that a small public-data feature stack plus a classical
tree-based model produces a defensible within-region prospectivity
map.

## Interesting finding 2: the cross-region transfer test failed informatively

When we applied the Mother Lode-trained model to the Klamath
Mountains orogenic-gold belt — geologically the closest analogue
province in California — the transfer AUC dropped to **0.58**,
barely above random. This is not a failure of the modeling pipeline
but a result that captures something real about regional
prospectivity: a model fit on the geophysical and geochemical
signature of the Mother Lode's specific Sierra Nevada batholith and
surrounding metamorphic terranes does not generalize to the
Klamath's accreted-terrane geology, even for the same deposit type.

We document this finding alongside the Mother Lode model rather
than hiding it, because explicit acknowledgment of where the model
does not transfer is more useful to the field than a single headline
AUC. The pattern recurs across the project: within-region numbers
look strong; cross-region transfer is much harder than the published
literature implies; the gap between the two is the gap between
"this model can highlight where to drill in a region we have
already characterized" and "this model can predict deposits in a
region nobody has drilled yet."

## Files and reproduction

- Pipeline driver: [scripts/motherlode/phase3.py](../../scripts/motherlode/phase3.py)
- Region config: [src/ai_minerals/regions/motherlode.py](../../src/ai_minerals/regions/motherlode.py)
- Cox-Singer label cleanup: [scripts/motherlode/v3p1_groupCD.py](../../scripts/motherlode/v3p1_groupCD.py)
- v3.1 improvements writeup: [notebooks/motherlode/v3p1_improvements.qmd](../../notebooks/motherlode/v3p1_improvements.qmd)

## Sources

- USGS, Bouguer gravity anomaly map of the conterminous United States.
- USGS, magnetic anomaly map of the conterminous United States.
- USGS, National Geochemical Database (NGDB) stream-sediment chemistry.
- USGS, State Geologic Map Compilation (SGMC).
- USGS, Mineral Resources Data System (MRDS).
- USGS, 1 arc-second digital elevation model.
- Cox, D. P. and Singer, D. A. (eds), 1986. Mineral deposit models. USGS Bulletin 1693.
